narrative:
Mike Johnson represents Louisiana's 4th Congressional District and serves as Speaker of the House, making him the single most powerful person in determining which bills reach the House floor for a vote. A constitutional lawyer by training and a deeply conservative evangelical Christian, Johnson was a relative backbencher before his surprise elevation to the speakership in October 2023, when the Republican conference turned to him after burning through several higher-profile candidates [1]. He arrived in the big chair with a thin fundraising record, a modest personal net worth, and a knack for sounding reasonable while presiding over an increasingly ungovernable caucus.

On the stock trading ban -- one of the most broadly popular items in the 80% Bill -- Johnson has performed an exquisite two-step. In May 2025 he told reporters he was "in favor of it" because "I don't think we should have any appearance of impropriety here," but conspicuously declined to commit to scheduling a floor vote [2]. By late 2025, The Intercept reported that advocates worried Johnson was simply stalling, noting that the Speaker could have gone directly to markup if he were genuinely supportive [3]. Johnson later flipped to outright opposition, arguing that a ban might deter "good people" from running for office [4]. On the Epstein files, he followed a near-identical pattern: he called the effort a "shiny object" and delayed scheduling a vote for months, only to pivot and personally vote yes once a bipartisan discharge petition made the measure unstoppable and President Trump publicly endorsed it [5]. The pattern is consistent -- express sympathy for popular reforms, delay until external pressure becomes irresistible, then claim credit.

On economic and social safety-net issues, Johnson's record as chairman of the Republican Study Committee from 2019 to 2021 tells the real story. The RSC budget blueprints he championed proposed roughly $750 billion in Social Security cuts, $2 trillion in Medicare cuts, and $3 trillion in Medicaid and ACA reductions, while endorsing a gradual increase to the retirement age to 69 [6]. He voted against the Inflation Reduction Act, which contained the first-ever Medicare drug price negotiation provisions, while his RSC budgets called for increased defense spending at a time when the Pentagon has failed seven consecutive independent audits [7]. On paid family leave, Johnson used a procedural maneuver in 2025 to block a bipartisan bill that would have allowed House members to vote remotely while on parental leave, wrapping it in unrelated legislation to kill it [8]. He has signed the U.S. Term Limits pledge, one of his few alignments with the 80% Bill agenda [9].

The money side of the ledger illuminates the rest. Johnson's personal campaign committee raised modestly before the speakership, but becoming Speaker transformed him into a fundraising conduit for the entire Republican conference. His leadership PAC, the American Revival PAC, raised $4.14 million in the 2023-2024 cycle [1]. His top industry donors over his career include oil and gas interests, which have contributed over $330,000, reflecting the petrochemical dominance of his northwest Louisiana district [10]. AIPAC donated $104,000 to Johnson following the Gaza war and his push to pass the Israel aid package [11]. Speaker-aligned groups brought in $16 million in a single 10-day stretch in late 2023 [12].

The bottom line on Mike Johnson is that he holds the keys to the House floor and has used them primarily to lock the door. He verbally supports the stock trading ban but will not schedule a vote; he championed budgets that would gut Social Security and Medicare; he blocked parental leave on a procedural technicality. For the 80% Bill, the Speaker is not simply a "no" vote -- he is the gatekeeper who decides whether these overwhelmingly popular measures ever get a vote at all.

Rep. Dusty Johnson has carved out an unusual niche in the Republican caucus: the guy who actually supports one of the 80% Bill's policies and uses it as a shield against scrutiny on the other nineteen. Johnson has been a genuine champion of banning congressional stock trading, introducing the TRUST in Congress Act alongside bipartisan co-sponsors and proudly noting he hasn't personally traded a single stock since taking office in 2019. Give the man a gold star. Now let's talk about the other ninety-five percent of his record.

As a member of the self-congratulatory "Problem Solvers Caucus," Johnson's brand is moderation — the political equivalent of ordering a side salad with your double bacon cheeseburger. His 2024 campaign was funded roughly 35% by PAC contributions, with top donor Moyle Petroleum writing checks worth $58,300 through his victory committee. For a guy representing a state where family farms are being swallowed by corporate agriculture, that petroleum money sure does flow freely. Johnson sits on the House Agriculture Committee, where he's championed the Farm Bill and even called for Bill Gates to testify about his massive farmland purchases — a populist flourish that conveniently ignores the domestic corporate consolidation that actually threatens South Dakota ranchers.

On the 80% Bill's other planks, Johnson's record ranges from silent to hostile. He voted against the Respect for Marriage Act, has taken no meaningful action on dark money disclosure, and has never co-sponsored term limits legislation despite representing a state where people genuinely believe in citizen legislators. Pentagon audit? No urgency. Medicare drug negotiation? Not his department. Paid family leave? The free market will sort it out, presumably. He did vote for an antitrust package in 2022, cracking down on anti-competitive corporate behavior — but that bipartisan moment was the exception that proves the rule.

Johnson's genius is the politics of the partial credit. He picks one wildly popular issue — stock trading — rides it hard, and hopes nobody notices that his donor list reads like a who's-who of the industries the 80% Bill would regulate. He's the congressional equivalent of a restaurant that advertises "farm-to-table" but sources everything from Sysco. South Dakotans deserve a representative who fights for all twenty policies their neighbors support, not just the one that makes for a good press release.

Dr. John Joyce spent three decades as a dermatologist in Altoona before trading his white coat for a congressional seat in 2019, proving that diagnosing skin conditions and diagnosing what ails Washington require entirely different skill sets — and he only mastered the first one. The good doctor now chairs the Oversight and Investigations Subcommittee of the House Energy and Commerce Committee [1], which means he oversees the very pharmaceutical and health industries that generously fund his campaigns. It is the legislative equivalent of a dermatologist prescribing his own skincare line.

Joyce voted against the Inflation Reduction Act, which included Medicare drug price negotiation — a policy supported by nearly 90% of Americans [2]. To his credit, he did introduce the Expanding Seniors' Access to Lower Cost Medicines Act to help seniors access cheaper biosimilar drugs [3]. But opposing the landmark bill that actually passed while championing a narrower alternative is a classic congressional move: look busy without disturbing the donors. The pharmaceutical and health products industry has been a top contributor to Energy and Commerce Committee members for years, and Joyce sits right in the sweet spot of that money pipeline [4].

On campaign finance reform, Joyce voted against H.R. 1, the For the People Act, which bundled the DISCLOSE Act, anti-gerrymandering measures, and small-dollar donation matching into one sweeping package [5]. His stated objection? That it would let "tax dollars fund political campaigns." Never mind that the bill would have also banned dark money, required super PAC disclosure, and curbed corporate PAC influence — all provisions supported by vast bipartisan majorities of voters. Opposing reform while accepting PAC money is like a dermatologist warning patients about sun damage while running a tanning salon.

Joyce also objected to the certification of the 2020 Electoral College results, voting against certifying electors from both Arizona and Pennsylvania on January 6, 2021 [6]. He announced his intention to "stand up for President Trump" before the Capitol was even breached, framing Pennsylvania's lawful mail-in voting expansion as some kind of constitutional crisis. The Fair Elections plank of the 80% Bill calls for protecting voting rights — Joyce chose the opposite.

On the economic planks, Joyce has been a reliable vote against taxing the ultra-wealthy and closing corporate tax loopholes. He voted against the Ukraine aid package in 2024 and supported banning cluster munitions transfers [7], which might suggest End Forever Wars sympathies — except his votes were less about peace and more about MAGA base maintenance. On stock trading bans, term limits, paid family leave, right to repair, and junk fee bans, Joyce has been conspicuously silent, which in Washington is how you say "no" without creating a paper trail.

The 80% Bill exists because policies like Medicare drug negotiation, dark money bans, and congressional stock trading restrictions enjoy overwhelming public support but die in committees chaired by people exactly like John Joyce — well-credentialed professionals who arrived in Washington promising to fix things and instead learned to profit from the dysfunction.

Ronny Jackson's journey from White House physician to Texas congressman is the kind of career arc that makes you wonder if Washington runs on Ambien — which, according to a Pentagon inspector general report, Jackson himself was taking while on duty as the president's doctor. The 2021 investigation found that he berated subordinates, made "sexual and denigrating statements" about a female colleague, drank alcohol inappropriately with staff, and consumed prescription sleep medication while responsible for the leader of the free world's medical care. The Navy demoted him from rear admiral to captain in 2022. Then, in September 2025, the Trump administration quietly reversed the demotion because in this timeline, accountability has a shelf life.

Before Congress, Jackson was best known for his 2018 press conference declaring that Donald Trump was in "excellent health" and could live to be 200 years old if he ate better — a medical assessment so detached from observable reality that it should have come with a fiction disclaimer. Trump rewarded this loyalty by nominating Jackson for VA Secretary, a bid that collapsed under bipartisan opposition after the misconduct allegations surfaced. Undeterred, Jackson ran for Congress in the Texas Panhandle's deep-red 13th District, where the only qualification needed is a Trump endorsement and a pulse.

Jackson now sits on Armed Services, Foreign Affairs, and the Permanent Select Committee on Intelligence — committees overseeing the very Pentagon apparatus that investigated and demoted him. On the 80% Bill scorecard, his record is a wasteland. He voted against certifying the 2020 election results from Arizona and Pennsylvania on January 6, 2021, hours after the Capitol insurrection. Banning stock trading, auditing the Pentagon, ending dark money, term limits, paid family leave — Jackson has shown zero interest in any of it.

His campaign finance record is its own comedy. The House Ethics Committee investigated Jackson for spending $11,928 in campaign funds at the Amarillo Club, a private dining establishment, between 2020 and 2024. Federal law explicitly prohibits using campaign money for club memberships. Jackson's defense was that the club was used for "meetings," and in December 2024, the Ethics Committee cleared him while noting his campaign did not "entirely adhere" to finance regulations — a phrase so diplomatically hollow it could have been written by Jackson's own medical staff.

The pattern with Ronny Jackson is simple: unwavering loyalty to one man, total indifference to the 80 percent of Americans who agree on commonsense reform. He represents a district where cattle outnumber contested elections, and he legislates accordingly — which is to say, he barely legislates at all.

Sara Jacobs represents California's 51st Congressional District, anchored in San Diego, and she would like you to know she is not your typical congresswoman. At 36, she is among the youngest members of the House. She is also, with an estimated net worth of $85 million, one of the richest -- a fortune inherited from her grandfather Irwin Jacobs, co-founder of Qualcomm [1][2]. She sits on both the House Armed Services Committee and the House Foreign Affairs Committee, the youngest member on each, and she holds a master's degree from Harvard Kennedy School [3]. Her campaign website proudly declares she accepts no corporate donations or contributions from federal lobbyists [4]. This is, of course, considerably easier when your grandfather can write seven-figure checks to a super PAC bearing your name.

On the substance of the 80% Bill, Jacobs is remarkably well-aligned. She co-sponsored the bipartisan Restore Trust in Congress Act to ban members and their families from trading individual stocks, pointedly noting that "if I can co-sponsor this as one of the richest Members of Congress, then every Member should too" [5]. She is an original co-sponsor of Medicare for All and supports Medicare drug price negotiation [6]. She backs repealing outdated AUMFs and has been a vocal advocate for reclaiming congressional war powers, introducing the bipartisan Conflict Prevention Act and the Block the Bombs Act to withhold offensive weapons transfers to Israel [7][8]. She supports overturning Citizens United, has called for a constitutional amendment to get corporate money out of politics, and wants to ban members from lobbying after leaving office [4]. She championed the Build Back Better Act's paid family leave provisions and supports protecting and expanding Social Security [9][10]. She even introduced legislation to fund affordable childcare that she cheerfully described as a bill "to decrease her inheritance" [11].

The trouble, as always, is the money. In the 2020 cycle, Jacobs was the fifth most self-funded candidate in the country, pouring $6.9 million of family wealth into her campaign -- fully 90% of her total fundraising [12]. Her billionaire grandparents simultaneously bankrolled a super PAC that attacked her progressive opponent Georgette Gomez, prompting Gomez's campaign strategist to call the notion that the PAC was "independent from Sara Jacobs" laughable [13]. In 2024, Qualcomm Inc. was the top donor to the Sara Jacobs House Victory Fund at $199,400, a cozy arrangement for someone sitting on the Armed Services Committee in a district dominated by defense and telecom contractors [14]. Jacobs holds up to $25 million in Qualcomm stock, which she disclosed to the House clerk -- stock she would presumably have to divest if the very ban she champions ever passed [1].

The contradictions are almost too perfect. Here is a woman born into the Qualcomm dynasty who genuinely pushes to ban congressional stock trading, close dark money loopholes, and tax the ultra-wealthy. She introduced a bill to restrict the revolving door between Congress and arms lobbying alongside a Republican co-sponsor [15]. But she bought her seat with dynastic wealth, represents a district thick with defense industry interests, and her family's telecom empire remains her largest financial entanglement. Jacobs is the rare case where the messenger's credibility problem does not stem from opposing the reforms -- she supports nearly all of them -- but from embodying the very system of inherited wealth and influence that makes those reforms necessary in the first place.

John James is a man who has never met a biographical detail he couldn't turn into a campaign slogan. A West Point graduate, Army Ranger, and Apache helicopter pilot who logged over 750 combat hours in Iraq, James parlayed his military credentials into a family business career and then into politics with the practiced ease of someone who understood that "combat veteran" polls better than "logistics CEO" [1]. After two failed Senate bids in 2018 and 2020 — the latter of which he declined to concede for more than three weeks while baselessly alleging voting irregularities — he found a more hospitable landing zone in Michigan's newly drawn 10th Congressional District, winning the seat in 2022 [2].

The family business chapter of James's biography is instructive. After leaving the Army in 2012, he joined James Group International, the Detroit-based supply chain management company founded by his father, eventually becoming president while also serving as CEO of its subsidiary, Renaissance Global Logistics [3]. The company coordinates shipment of American auto parts to assembly plants worldwide, including to Changan Ford Automobile Co., a joint venture involving a Chinese state-owned automaker — a business relationship that sat somewhat awkwardly alongside James's campaign trail rhetoric about getting tough on China [4]. Renaissance also received $1.75 million in tax breaks from the Michigan Economic Development Corporation on the condition it create 108 new jobs, though the details of whether those jobs materialized have been a subject of scrutiny [5].

In Congress, James has distinguished himself primarily by his ambition to leave it. He announced his candidacy for Michigan governor in April 2025, joining a crowded Republican primary where he quickly became the frontrunner [6]. His attendance record reflects this divided attention: from January 2023 through early 2026, he missed 4.3 percent of roll call votes, more than double the median rate for sitting representatives, suggesting that the 10th District's constituents have been paying for a full-time representative and receiving something closer to a part-time one [7].

On the policy questions that animate the 80% Bill, James occupies the standard Republican position of supporting market-based healthcare reform while opposing single-payer systems, advocating for deregulation, and framing his agenda in the language of freedom and transparency without always delivering the substance [8]. He has supported ethics reform rhetoric but has not been a leading voice on banning congressional stock trading, closing dark money loopholes, or auditing the Pentagon — positions that enjoy overwhelming public support across party lines. His "Freedom Agenda" gubernatorial platform emphasizes ending fraud and waste, a goal that is difficult to argue with and equally difficult to measure, which is perhaps the point.

Jonathan L. Jackson represents Illinois's 1st Congressional District, the South Side Chicago seat that Bobby Rush held for three decades before retiring in 2022. Jackson won the open primary as a progressive, endorsed by Bernie Sanders, and cruised to a general election victory with nearly 66 percent of the vote [1]. His résumé is as layered as his last name is loaded: son of the Reverend Jesse Jackson, godson of Martin Luther King Jr., former national spokesman for the Rainbow/PUSH Coalition, Northwestern MBA, and co-owner of River North Sales and Service, an Anheuser-Busch beer distributorship he and his brother Yusef acquired in 1998 under circumstances that invited accusations of nepotism given their father's earlier boycott of the brewery [2]. He sits on the Agriculture and Foreign Affairs committees, where he has focused on protecting SNAP benefits and sanctioning Houthi rebels, respectively [3].

On the 80% Bill scorecard, Jackson talks a good progressive game. He joined the Congressional Progressive Caucus and the Congressional Black Caucus, scored 95 percent on the AFL-CIO's 2024 scorecard, and has vocally defended Social Security, Medicare, and Medicaid against Republican budget cuts [4][5]. He has championed food security and fought to preserve SNAP funding within the Farm Bill, arguing that roughly 80 percent of its spending goes to nutrition assistance [3]. His committee work on agriculture gives him a natural lane on protecting U.S. farmland, though specific legislative action on that front remains thin.

Where Jackson's progressive brand runs into trouble is on the issue of congressional stock trading — one of the 80% Bill's most universally popular planks. Jackson has violated the STOCK Act not once but twice: first in 2023, when he was late disclosing up to $300,000 in stock trades from a joint trust, and again in 2025, when he failed to timely report 31 separate trades by his wife in stocks including Amazon, Eli Lilly, Goldman Sachs, IBM, Meta, and T-Mobile, worth between $30,000 and $450,000 [6]. The penalty each time was a $200 fine, the congressional equivalent of a parking ticket. Then came the Palantir episode: in December 2025, Jackson purchased between $15,001 and $50,000 in stock in Palantir Technologies — the data analytics company holding a $30 million contract with ICE to streamline deportations — while simultaneously criticizing Trump's immigration enforcement on the House floor [7]. He sold the shares less than a month later, calling the investment "absolutely embarrassing" and blaming his financial advisor [7]. One wonders how many embarrassing investments the advisor has to make before losing the account.

Jackson's campaign finance picture is relatively modest for a safe-seat Democrat, but the pattern is familiar: labor unions and leadership PACs figure prominently among his donors [8]. His seat in deep-blue IL-1 means he faces no meaningful general election threat, which makes his reluctance to cosponsor structural reforms like a congressional stock trading ban, a lifetime lobbying ban, or term limits all the more conspicuous. When the political cost of doing the right thing is essentially zero, choosing not to act is itself a statement.

Jackson arrived in Congress with a storied name, a progressive endorsement, and a district that would let him be as bold as he claims to want to be. Instead, he has delivered the familiar contradiction: populist rhetoric paired with personal financial behavior that undermines it. A congressman who cannot even follow the existing, toothless disclosure rules on stock trading is an unlikely champion for banning the practice altogether — but that is precisely the champion his constituents need him to be.

Julie Johnson, the freshman Democrat from Texas's 32nd district, ran for Congress as a civil rights attorney who would fight for working families. She even cosponsored a bill to ban congressional stock trading in December 2025 and introduced an amendment to strengthen the GOP version. Noble stuff. Except Johnson apparently forgot to tell her "independent third-party money managers" about her deeply held convictions, because her first year in office produced one of the most active stock trading portfolios in the entire House.

In January 2025, just days before Trump's inauguration, Johnson purchased shares of Palantir Technologies — the surveillance firm that serves as ICE's digital backbone for tracking and deporting immigrants. This is the same ICE that Johnson has publicly denounced. Palantir's government contracts nearly doubled from $541 million to $970 million as deportation efforts ramped up, and Johnson's shares rode that wave before she sold them by June 2025. She insists she made only $90 in profit, which is either a testament to spectacularly bad timing or spectacularly creative accounting.

But the Palantir episode was merely the appetizer. On April 1 and April 7, 2025 — straddling Trump's "Liberation Day" tariff announcement on April 2 — Johnson executed a staggering 132 separate stock sale transactions, dumping between $218,000 and $2.22 million worth of holdings in Amazon, Apple, Boeing, Meta, Microsoft, Chevron, ExxonMobil, and more. The market promptly cratered. Johnson's defense? The same one every congressional stock trader reaches for like a security blanket: "I have money managers that take care of all of it." Apparently those money managers have an uncanny sense of geopolitical timing.

Johnson also accepted corporate PAC money during her primary campaign, drawing pointed criticism from Colin Allred, who noted he never took "a dime of corporate PAC money" in six years. She sits on the House Homeland Security Committee, giving her access to sensitive intelligence about the very tech and defense companies whose stocks she was frantically trading. On the 80% Bill priorities — banning stock trading, ending dark money, closing corporate PAC loopholes — Johnson talks a progressive game while her brokerage account tells a decidedly different story. The first openly lesbian member of Congress from a Southern state deserves credit for breaking barriers, but the barrier between her portfolio and her committee work could use some reinforcement.

Brian Jack arrived in Congress in January 2025 not as an outsider storming the gates but as the insider who already had the keys. Before representing Georgia's 3rd Congressional District, Jack served as White House Political Director under Donald Trump from 2019 to 2021, the man responsible for managing the administration's relationships with every member of Congress [1]. Before that, he worked as a political analyst at AIPAC, the powerful pro-Israel lobbying organization, where he liaised directly with congressional candidates [2]. His career trajectory -- from AIPAC to the RNC to the Trump White House to a congressional seat won with Trump's personal endorsement -- is less a political biography than a Washington networking masterclass. Within weeks of his election, his freshman colleagues chose him to sit on the Republican Steering Committee, the powerful panel that distributes committee assignments, and by all accounts he delivered: nine freshmen landed on the most prestigious committees, the highest share since the 1990s [3].

Jack now sits on the Rules Committee, the Oversight and Government Reform Committee, and the Small Business Committee, where he chairs the Subcommittee on Innovation, Entrepreneurship, and Workforce Development [4]. His Oversight seat places him on the subcommittee for Delivering on Government Efficiency -- the congressional counterpart to the DOGE initiative -- giving him a front-row seat to the Trump administration's efforts to slash the federal workforce [5]. He has signed on to DOGE subcommittee letters and voiced support for rooting out "waste, fraud, and abuse," adopting the language of fiscal discipline that sounds like it might lead to auditing the Pentagon but somehow never does.

On the 80% Bill's twenty planks, Jack's record is short but telling. He supports term limits and opposes earmarks, positions endorsed by the Club for Growth, which invested nearly $500,000 in his primary campaign [6]. He has pledged not to support continuing resolutions or omnibus spending bills, which is the kind of promise that sounds principled until you realize it mainly functions as leverage for deeper spending cuts rather than for accountability measures like Pentagon audits or Social Security funding. On banning congressional stock trading, ending dark money, the DISCLOSE Act, Medicare drug negotiation, paid family leave, right to repair, banning junk fees, and banning corporate purchases of single-family homes, Jack has taken no public position -- which, for a freshman with his connections, is itself a position. He is a free-market conservative who opposes "government picking winners and losers" [7], a philosophy that conveniently rules out most of the 80% Bill's consumer-protection and anti-corruption provisions.

Follow the money and the pattern crystallizes. Jack raised over $600,000 in his first three weeks as a candidate [8]. The Club for Growth PAC endorsed him and ran ads against his primary opponent [6]. His career has been steeped in the institutional donor class -- AIPAC, the RNC, Trump's fundraising apparatus -- and his campaign finance profile reflects it. He represents a district that gave Trump roughly two-thirds of its vote, and he has governed accordingly: loyal to the party infrastructure, attentive to the donor networks that elevated him, and silent on the popular reforms that might disrupt either. The 80% Bill asks whether representatives will side with the 80% of Americans who support these proposals or with the system that got them elected. Brian Jack, more than most freshmen, is the system.

Jim Justice is the richest member of the United States Senate — or the most indebted, depending on which year you ask Forbes. Once valued at $1.9 billion, Justice's fortune has cratered so spectacularly that by 2025 Forbes estimated his net worth had fallen below zero, weighed down by over $1 billion in debt. He owes the IRS $5.2 million in back personal taxes. His family's Greenbrier resort had $1.4 million in state tax liens filed against it. His coal companies owe $600,000 in unpaid mine safety fines from a settlement they agreed to in 2020 but never bothered to finish paying. And yet, the people of West Virginia looked at this financial track record and said: "Yes, please send this man to Washington to manage our tax dollars."

Justice's coal empire is less an empire and more a cautionary tale with a congressional salary. The federal government has sued his companies repeatedly for environmental violations, seeking $7.6 million in unpaid fines. In 2023, the DOJ cited more than 130 violations and 40 serious enforcement orders issued against Justice mines between 2018 and 2022. His attorneys have argued in court that the companies literally cannot afford to pay the remaining safety fines — a bold defense from a man who bought the Greenbrier resort out of bankruptcy for $20.1 million and once sold his coal operations to a Russian company called Mechel, only to buy them back for pennies on the dollar when Mechel went under.

As governor, Justice turned the Greenbrier into a masterclass in conflicts of interest. He held his inaugural ball there, routing over $1 million in inaugural funds to his own hotel corporation. State agencies spent more than $106,000 on meals and lodging at the resort despite a supposed "moratorium" on government spending there. He pressed Greenbrier County commissioners for $10 million in taxpayer funds for resort-related projects. He promised to put the resort in a blind trust, then quietly abandoned the idea.

On the 80% Bill's twenty policies, Justice is a freshman senator whose voting record is still taking shape, but the pattern is clear. He has shown zero interest in banning stock trading, ending dark money, auditing the Pentagon, or reforming campaign finance. He missed 4.4% of roll call votes in his first year — worse than the Senate median. When you owe more money than some countries' GDP and your coal mines are racking up safety violations while you sit on Capitol Hill, "draining the swamp" takes on a deeply personal irony. Jim Justice isn't in the swamp — he is the swamp, and it's got unpaid liens on it.
